title: SSH Brute Force Attack
indicators: Multiple failed SSH login attempts from a single IP
Login attempts across many usernames in short time
Source IP from unusual geolocation
Attempts targeting root or admin accounts
explanation: An SSH brute force attack occurs when an attacker repeatedly attempts to authenticate via SSH using automated tools, cycling through password lists or common credentials. Multiple failed logins from a single source IP in a short window is the primary indicator.
mitigation: Block the source IP at the firewall immediately
Enable Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) on all SSH access
Configure fail2ban or equivalent to auto-ban IPs after 5 failed attempts
Restrict SSH access to known IP ranges via allowlist
Disable password-based SSH auth; enforce key-based auth only
Monitor affected user accounts for signs of successful compromise
detection: Configure SIEM rule: >5 failed SSH logins from same source IP within 60 seconds. Correlate with successful login events from same IP to detect if brute force succeeded.

title: Port Scanning / Reconnaissance
indicators: Sequential connection attempts to many ports on a single host
SYN packets without completing TCP handshake
Connections to known honeypot ports
High volume of ICMP packets from single source
explanation: Port scanning is a reconnaissance technique where an attacker probes a host to discover open services. It often precedes an actual attack and indicates the attacker is mapping the network.
mitigation: Block source IP if scanning is aggressive
Deploy IDS/IPS rules to detect and alert on scan patterns
Ensure firewall rules expose only required ports
Deploy honeypot ports to detect and attribute scans
detection: Alert on: >20 unique destination ports from single source IP within 30 seconds. Separate rules for SYN-only scans and full-connect scans.

title: Phishing / Credential Harvesting
indicators: User clicked link in suspicious email
DNS query to newly registered or typosquat domain
Browser navigated to credential harvesting page
POST request with form data to unknown external host
explanation: Phishing attacks trick users into submitting credentials or downloading malware via deceptive emails or websites. Credential harvesting pages mimic legitimate login portals.
mitigation: Immediately reset credentials for affected accounts
Block the phishing domain at DNS/proxy level
Force MFA re-enrollment for impacted users
Alert user and conduct awareness refresher
Scan endpoint for malware download from phishing link
detection: Correlate email gateway alerts with proxy/DNS logs. Alert on: user visited domain registered < 30 days ago AND submitted form data.

title: Privilege Escalation Attempt
indicators: Unexpected sudo or su command execution
Process running as root spawned by non-root parent
Access to /etc/passwd or /etc/shadow outside maintenance window
New user added to sudoers or admin group
explanation: Privilege escalation occurs when an attacker or malware gains elevated system rights beyond their initial foothold, enabling deeper system access, persistence, and data exfiltration.
mitigation: Immediately revoke elevated access from compromised account
Kill suspicious processes running with escalated privileges
Audit sudoers file and remove unauthorized entries
Isolate affected host from network pending investigation
Conduct full forensic review of endpoint
detection: Alert on: sudo commands by accounts not in approved sudo users list, or process privilege change detected by EDR outside change window.

title: Data Exfiltration
indicators: Unusually large outbound data transfer
Connections to cloud storage (Dropbox, Mega) outside business hours
DNS queries with abnormally long subdomain labels (DNS tunneling)
Compressed archive files created and immediately transferred
explanation: Data exfiltration is the unauthorized transfer of data from the organization's network. Attackers often use encrypted channels, DNS tunneling, or legitimate cloud services to evade detection.
mitigation: Block outbound connection to identified exfil destination immediately
Preserve all network flow logs for forensics
Identify and contain the source host
Engage incident response team and legal/compliance
Notify DPO if personal data is involved (GDPR/PDPA)
detection: Alert on: outbound transfer >500MB to single destination within 1 hour, or DNS query subdomain length >50 chars. Baseline normal egress per host for anomaly detection.

title: Malware / Ransomware Execution
indicators: Mass file rename events with unknown extension
Ransom note file created on desktop or shared drives
Sudden spike in disk I/O from unexpected process
Outbound connection to known C2 IP/domain
explanation: Ransomware encrypts files and demands payment for decryption keys. Execution typically follows an initial access vector (phishing, exploit) and may spread laterally across the network before triggering encryption.
mitigation: Immediately isolate infected host from network
Do NOT pay ransom without security team approval
Identify patient zero and initial access vector
Restore from clean offline backup after full remediation
Report to CERT and law enforcement if required
detection: Alert on: >100 file rename events per minute from single process, or process writing to >10 unique directory paths within 30 seconds.
